            DIRECTIVE:
            # blueprint-writer — Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex, stage (iv) balancing guidance

## Scope (edit ONLY this chapter)
`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex`, the proof body of
`\begin{lemma}\label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}` (the d.2 stalk–tensor commutation),
**stage (iv) "The reverse map"** (currently around lines 1969–1986).

## Problem to fix (blueprint-reviewer ts236 must-fix #1)
Stage (iv) currently ends with the single sentence: "R_x-bilinearity reduces to the
germ–scalar compatibility of (i)/(iii)." A prover reading only the blueprint hit a wall
on the balancing condition `revBihom_balanced` (i.e. the bilinear map's `R_x`-balancing
`revBihom (r·a) b = revBihom a (r·b)`) precisely because the prose does not warn about
the carrier-duality obstacle nor name the technique that avoids it.

## Required addition (mathematical prose only — NO Lean tactic strings)
Expand the stage-(iv) bilinearity/balancing discussion so it conveys, in textbook prose
restated in the project's notation, the following three mathematical points. The math
content is the substance; phrase it as exposition, not as a Lean recipe:

1. **Prove the balancing at the STALK level, not the section level.** The `R_x`-balancing
   of the reverse bilinear map should be established as the identity
   `revBihom (germ_x r · a) b = (germ_x r) · revBihom a b` where the scalar `germ_x r`
   stays in the stalk ring `R_x = R.stalk x` throughout. The germ–scalar compatibility
   `germ_x (r · s) = (germ_x r) · (germ_x s)` (the same germ–scalar compatibility used in
   stages (i) and (iii)) is what makes this hold. Working at the stalk level keeps every
   scalar action over `R_x`.

2. **Why one must NOT reduce to a section-level identity.** If instead one passes the
   scalar through the presheaf restriction at a common neighbourhood `W` (using the
   module structure map of the presheaf-of-modules `A`), the resulting section-level
   identity must be read in `A(W) ⊗_{R(W)} B(W)`, and there the scalar action is the one
   that the presheaf-of-modules structure produces via restriction of scalars — it lives
   over the `RingCat` carrier `(R ∘ forget₂)(W)`, NOT over the `CommRingCat` carrier
   `R(W)` that annotates the section tensor. This is the same CommRingCat-vs-RingCat
   carrier-duality obstacle already met in stage (iii); at the section level it is
   compounded by an extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive "move the scalar
   across the tensor" step does not apply directly. The stage-(iv) balancing should
   therefore be done at the stalk level (point 1), avoiding this section-level wrapper.

3. **The carrier-duality bridge is the same one used in stage (iii).** Where a
   `CommRingCat`-vs-`RingCat` carrier identification is unavoidable, it is the identical
   canonical bridge already invoked for the stage-(iii) `R_x`-linearity packaging
   (the `stalkTensorDescU_smul`/`stalkTensorLinearMap` step, `\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}`):
   the two carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
   bridge resolves the obstruction here. Cross-reference stage (iii) so the reader sees
   the balancing reuses that resolved technique rather than re-deriving it.

Keep the existing five-stage structure and the Stacks SOURCE QUOTE intact. This is an
expansion of the stage-(iv) prose only. Do NOT add/remove any `\leanok`/`\mathlibok`
markers (the deterministic sync owns `\leanok`). Do NOT introduce Lean tactic names as
the content — name the mathematical objects (germ–scalar compatibility, stalk ring `R_x`,
the carrier identification) and cross-reference stage (iii); you may mention the Lean
identifier `germ_smul` once as the germ–scalar compatibility lemma if helpful, but the
prose must stand as mathematics.

## Out of scope
Do not touch any other lemma block, the dead duplicate whisker lemma, or the FlatBaseChange
chapter.

3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	dispatcher_notes: |
9	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
10	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
11	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
12	    multi-route coverage.
13	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
14	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
15	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
16	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
17	    what the directive lists.
18	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` or ``\mathlibok``
19	    markers** (no "after writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no
20	    "verification: confirm `\leanok` is present"). The writer's
21	    descriptor forbids it — ``\leanok`` is managed by the
22	    deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase, ``\mathlibok`` by the review
23	    agent. A directive that asks the writer to add markers puts it in
24	    a rule conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the
25	    "verification" instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok
26	    runs. Leave marker concerns out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
28	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
29	    writer with TWO globs:
30	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
31	      --write-domain 'references/**'
32	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
33	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
34	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
35	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
36	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
37	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
38	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
39	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
40	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
41	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
42	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
43	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
44	    the writer fixed everything.
45	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying

47	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
48	    strategy-level issue.
49	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
50	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
51	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.
52	---
53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
107	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
108	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
109	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
110	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
111	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
112	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
113	\end{theorem}
114	
115	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
116	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
117	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
118	\begin{proof}
119	  \uses{thm:another_result}
120	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
121	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
122	\end{proof}
123	```
124	
125	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
126	
127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
130	
131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
133	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
134	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
135	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
136	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
137	
138	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
139	
140	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
141	
142	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
143	
144	**The hard rule, explicit:**
145	
146	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
147	
148	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
149	
150	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
151	
152	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
153	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
154	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
155	
156	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
157	
158	## Rules
159	
160	### What you CAN do
161	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
162	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
163	- Add `\uses{...}` cross-references.
164	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
165	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
166	
167	### What you MUST do
168	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
169	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
170	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
171	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
172	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
173	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
174	
175	### What you MUST NOT do
176	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
177	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
178	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` or `\mathlibok` markers.** Those are managed by the `sync_leanok` phase + the review agent — never by you.
179	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
180	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
181	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
182	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
183	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
184	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
185	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
186	
187	## Reading references
188	
189	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
190	
191	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
192	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
193	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
194	
195	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
196	
197	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
198	
199	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
200	
201	Conditions for dispatch:
202	
203	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
204	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
205	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
206	
207	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
208	
209	```
210	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
211	  --name reference-retriever \
212	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
213	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
214	  --write-domain 'references/**'
215	```
216	
217	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
218	
219	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
220	
221	## Workflow
222	
223	1. Read your directive completely.
224	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
225	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
226	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
227	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
228	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
229	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
230	8. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
231	
232	## Logging
233	
234	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-writer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
235	
236	```markdown
237	# Blueprint Writer Report
238	
239	## Slug
240	<slug>
241	
242	## Status
243	<COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE>
244	<If INCOMPLETE: which required items could not be written and why.>
245	
246	## Target chapter
247	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
248	
249	## Changes Made
250	- **Added definition** `\definition`/`\label{def:foo}`/`\lean{Foo.bar}` — <one line on what it captures>
251	- **Added theorem** `\theorem`/`\label{thm:foo}` — <one line on statement>
252	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>
253	- **Revised** `<existing label>` — <one line on what changed>
254	- ...
255

257	- `\uses{thm:bar}` added in proof of `\thm:foo` — verify `thm:bar` exists in <chapter or this same one>
258	- ...
259

261	<every local file under `references/` you OPENED and READ this session, with a one-line note on what you took from each. The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>
262	- `references/hartshorne-III-5.md` — verbatim quote for `\thm:smooth_criterion` (statement + proof).
263	- `references/stacks-tag-01V4.md` — verbatim quote for `\def:etale_morphism`.
264	
265	## Macros needed (if any)
266	- `\foo{...}` — used in <where>; needs definition in `macros/common.tex`. NOT added by me (out of write-domain).
267	
268	## Reference-retriever dispatches (if any)

270	- ...
271

273	- <any inconsistency I noticed in sibling chapters that the directive didn't cover>
274	- <any structural concern: e.g. "the chapter is now 800 lines, consider splitting">
275	- <any reference I had to guess at because the directive's reference section was incomplete>
276	
277	## Strategy-modifying findings
278	<populate this section ONLY if writing the chapter surfaced a need to
279	change the project's strategy itself — not just the chapter content.
280	Examples:
281	- A definition you were asked to write turns out to require a property
282	  that the strategy assumes is automatic but is not.
283	- A theorem you sketched is provable as stated, but its consequence
284	  used elsewhere in STRATEGY.md does not follow from it.
285	- A reference cited as authoritative actually requires a different
286	  setup than the strategy assumes.
287	
288	When this section is non-empty, the plan agent must update STRATEGY.md
289	before any further Lean work this iteration. Do NOT silently adapt the
290	chapter to paper over a strategy-level issue — surface it here.>
291	```
292	
293	## Return Value
294	
295	Your final assistant message:
296	
297	- One line: `<slug>: COMPLETE | INCOMPLETE — <one-sentence outcome>`
298	- The path to your full report.
299	
300	Keep the inline return short. The plan agent reads the full report.
301

1900	  product \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\).
1901	\end{lemma}
1902	
1903	\begin{proof}
1904	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:stalk_linear_map}
1905	  The stalk at \(x\) is the filtered colimit over the neighbourhoods \(U \ni x\) of
1906	  the sections functor, and sectionwise the tensor product is computed pointwise:
1907	  \((A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\)
1908	  (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj\_obj}\)). The neighbourhood system
1909	  \(\{U \ni x\}\) is cofiltered under inclusion, so the colimit is filtered. The
1910	  isomorphism is assembled in five named stages. Stages (i)--(ii) construct the
1911	  forward additive comparison map and its germ characterisation; stages (iii)--(v)
1912	  upgrade it to an \(R_x\)-linear map, construct the reverse map, and check mutual
1913	  inversion.
1914	
1915	  \emph{(i) Per-neighbourhood balanced bilinear map and its section-level descent}
1916	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorBilin}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}\)). For a fixed
1917	  neighbourhood \(U \ni x\) the assignment
1918	  \[
1919	    (a, b) \;\longmapsto\; \mathrm{germ}_x\,a \,\otimes\, \mathrm{germ}_x\,b,
1920	    \qquad a \in A(U),\ b \in B(U),
1921	  \]
1922	  is \(R(U)\)-balanced bilinear into \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\): it is additive in each
1923	  variable, and for \(r \in R(U)\) the two ways of moving \(r\) across the tensor agree
1924	  after passing to germs, since \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot a) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)
1925	  \cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\) and the stalk tensor is balanced over
1926	  \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) (the germ--scalar compatibility of
1927	  \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}). By the universal property of the relative tensor
1928	  product it descends to an additive map on the section module
1929	  \[
1930	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U \;\colon\;
1931	      (A \otimes^{p} B)(U) = A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)
1932	        \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1933	    \qquad a \otimes b \longmapsto \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b.
1934	  \]
1935	
1936	  \emph{(ii) Colimit descent to the forward comparison map}
1937	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\), \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). The
1938	  maps \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU}_U\) are compatible with the restriction maps of the
1939	  presheaf \(A \otimes^{p} B\) as \(U\) shrinks toward \(x\) (a smaller neighbourhood
1940	  computes the same germs), so they form a cocone over the filtered diagram
1941	  \(\{(A \otimes^{p} B)(U)\}_{U \ni x}\). The induced map out of the colimit is the
1942	  forward additive comparison map
1943	  \[
1944	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
1945	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\; A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x,
1946	  \]
1947	  characterised on germs of simple tensors by
1948	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\bigr) =
1949	  \mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes \mathrm{germ}_x b\)
1950	  (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)). This is the forward half of the asserted
1951	  isomorphism (\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}).
1952	
1953	  \emph{(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\)). The
1954	  additive map of (ii) is upgraded to an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear map: on a germ
1955	  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(U)\) one checks
1956	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \xi\bigr) =
1957	  (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc}(\xi)\) on germs of simple tensors
1958	  and extends by additivity. The one subtlety is a \emph{carrier-duality obstacle}:
1959	  the section-level tensor \(A(U) \otimes_{R(U)} B(U)\) is a module over the
1960	  \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(U)\), whereas the natural
1961	  scalar \(r : R(U)\) lives in the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier \(R(U)\); the two
1962	  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, so the
1963	  scalar-pulling lemma \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\) applies only after
1964	  inserting a canonical \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge between the
1965	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carriers. Once that bridge is
1966	  in place the linearity computation mirrors the germ-representative pattern used
1967	  for the d.1 stalk map \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) (\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}).
1968	
1969	  \emph{(iv) The reverse map.} The map
1970	  \[
1971	    A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \;\longrightarrow\; (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x
1972	  \]
1973	  is built by the universal property of the tensor product over \(R_x\) from the
1974	  \(R_x\)-bilinear map of the two stalks
1975	  \[
1976	    (\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b) \;\longmapsto\;
1977	      \mathrm{germ}_x\bigl(a|_{U \cap V} \otimes b|_{U \cap V}\bigr),
1978	  \]
1979	  where \(a \in A(U)\) and \(b \in B(V)\) are chosen representatives; this is a
1980	  \emph{nested} colimit descent, descending first the \(A\)-variable out of the
1981	  filtered colimit \(\{A(U)\}_{U \ni x}\) and then the \(B\)-variable out of
1982	  \(\{B(V)\}_{V \ni x}\), passing to the common refinement \(U \cap V\) to form the
1983	  simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
1984	  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
1985	  neighbourhood system, and \(R_x\)-bilinearity reduces to the germ--scalar
1986	  compatibility of (i)/(iii).
1987	
1988	  \emph{(v) Mutual inversion on germ generators} (\(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\)). The two
1989	  composites are checked to be identities on generators. The composite
1990	  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x
1991	  \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) is the identity on a simple tensor \(\mathrm{germ}_x a \otimes
1992	  \mathrm{germ}_x b\) by the germ characterisation \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDesc\_germ\_tmul}\)
1993	  of (ii), and extends to all of \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) by
1994	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\). The opposite composite
1995	  \((A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \to A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x \to (A \otimes^{p}
1996	  B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) is the identity on a germ \(\mathrm{germ}_x(a \otimes b)\) of
1997	  a simple tensor by the same characterisation; since germs generate the stalk and the
1998	  germ maps are jointly epimorphic (\(\mathtt{stalk\_hom\_ext}\)), together with
1999	  \(\mathtt{TensorProduct.induction\_on}\) applied to each section, the composite is the
2000	  identity. Bundling the \(R_x\)-linear forward map of (iii) with the reverse map of
2001	  (iv) and these two inversion identities yields the asserted isomorphism
2002	  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorIso}\).
2003	
2004	  Naturality in \(A, B\) is inherited from naturality of the sectionwise tensor and of
2005	  the germ maps. This is the presheaf-of-modules, varying-ring analogue of the Stacks
2006	  lemma above; the only subtlety beyond the ringed-space statement is that the ground
2007	  ring varies with \(U\), handled by the ``tensor of filtered colimits over the colimit
2008	  ring'' identification together with the carrier-duality bridge of (iii). Conceptually
2009	  the tensor product of modules commutes with filtered colimits in each variable and
2010	  the ground ring \(R(U)\) itself colimits to the stalk ring \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\),
2011	  which is the reason the forward map (ii) and the reverse map (iv) are mutually
2012	  inverse.
2013	\end{proof}
2014	
2015	\begin{lemma}
2016	
2017	\leanok
2018	[Forward stalk--tensor comparison map (d.2, partial)]
2019	  \label{lem:stalk_tensor_desc_forward}
2020	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.stalkTensorDesc}
2021	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
2022	  With \(R\), \(A\), \(B\), \(x\) as in \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}, there is
2023	  a canonical natural \emph{additive} comparison map
2024	  \[
2025	    \mathtt{stalkTensorDesc} \;\colon\;
2026	      (A \otimes^{p} B).\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\longrightarrow\;
2027	      A.\mathtt{stalk}\,x \;\otimes_{R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x}\; B.\mathtt{stalk}\,x,
2028	  \]

input.old_string:
simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen

input.new_string:
simple tensor and its germ. Well-definedness on germs (independence of the chosen
  representatives and of the neighbourhoods \(U, V\)) is exactly filteredness of the
  neighbourhood system.

  It remains to check that the reverse map is \(R_x\)-bilinear, and in particular that
  its underlying bilinear map is \emph{balanced} over \(R_x\), i.e. that pulling a scalar
  out of the left variable agrees with pulling it out of the right. The key point is to
  establish this balancing \emph{at the stalk level}, with every scalar living in the
  stalk ring \(R_x = R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\) throughout. Concretely, for a germ
  \(\mathrm{germ}_x r\) of a section \(r \in R(W)\) one verifies the identity
  \[
    \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr)
      \;=\; (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathtt{revBihom}\bigl(\mathrm{germ}_x a,\ \mathrm{germ}_x b\bigr),
  \]
  and symmetrically on the right variable, where the scalar \(\mathrm{germ}_x r \in R_x\)
  never leaves the stalk ring. This holds because of the germ--scalar compatibility
  \(\mathrm{germ}_x(r \cdot s) = (\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot(\mathrm{germ}_x s)\) of the germ
  map \(R(W) \to R_x\) (the Lean lemma \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\)) — the same germ--scalar
  compatibility already used in stages (i) and (iii). Multiplying a representative
  \(r \cdot a\) and then taking germs produces \((\mathrm{germ}_x r)\cdot \mathrm{germ}_x a\),
  so the action of \(r\) is transparently the \(R_x\)-action on the stalk and may be
  moved freely across the tensor of stalks, where balancedness over \(R_x\) is built into
  \(A_x \otimes_{R_x} B_x\). Because the whole computation takes place over the single
  ring \(R_x\), no carrier identification intervenes and the balancing follows directly.

  One must \emph{not} instead reduce this balancing to a section-level identity. If the
  scalar were pushed through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood \(W\) and
  the balancing read off inside the section module \(A(W) \otimes_{R(W)} B(W)\), then the
  scalar action appearing there is the one produced by the presheaf-of-modules structure
  of \(A\) via restriction of scalars: it lives over the \(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier
  \((R \circ \mathtt{forget_2})(W)\), \emph{not} over the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) carrier
  \(R(W)\) that annotates the section tensor. This is precisely the
  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier-duality obstacle already met in
  stage (iii), here compounded by the extra restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so the naive
  ``move the scalar across the tensor'' step (\(\mathtt{TensorProduct.smul\_tmul'}\)) does
  not apply at the section level without first inserting the carrier bridge. Working at the
  stalk level as above sidesteps this wrapper entirely.

  Where a \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-vs-\(\mathtt{RingCat}\) carrier identification is
  genuinely unavoidable, it is the \emph{same} canonical bridge already invoked for the
  stage-(iii) \(R_x\)-linearity packaging (the \(\mathtt{stalkTensorDescU\_smul}\)/%
  \(\mathtt{stalkTensorLinearMap}\) step, \cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}): the two
  carriers are canonically identified but not definitionally equal, and the same
  \(\mathtt{RingEquiv}\) bridge resolves the obstruction here. Thus the stage-(iv)
  balancing reuses the technique already resolved in stage (iii) rather than re-deriving
  it.

# Blueprint Writer Report

## Slug
d2-balancing

## Status
COMPLETE

## Target chapter
blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_TensorObjSubstrate.tex

## Changes Made
- **Revised** proof body of `\label{lem:stalk_tensor_commutation}`, stage (iv) "The reverse
  map" — replaced the single closing sentence ("...and `R_x`-bilinearity reduces to the
  germ–scalar compatibility of (i)/(iii).") with three expository paragraphs that convey, in
  textbook prose restated in the project's notation, the three required mathematical points:
  1. **Balancing proved at the STALK level.** Stated the explicit stalk-level identity
     `revBihom((germ_x r)·germ_x a, germ_x b) = (germ_x r)·revBihom(germ_x a, germ_x b)`
     with the scalar `germ_x r` staying in `R_x = R.stalk x` throughout, justified by the
     germ–scalar compatibility `germ_x(r·s) = (germ_x r)·(germ_x s)` (the same one used in
     stages (i)/(iii); the Lean identifier `germ_smul` is named once as that lemma, as the
     directive permitted). Emphasised that because the whole computation lives over the
     single ring `R_x`, no carrier identification intervenes.
  2. **Why NOT to reduce to a section-level identity.** Explained that pushing the scalar
     through the presheaf restriction to a common neighbourhood `W` forces the identity to
     be read in `A(W) ⊗_{R(W)} B(W)`, where the scalar action lives over the `RingCat`
     carrier `(R ∘ forget₂)(W)` rather than the `CommRingCat` carrier `R(W)` annotating the
     section tensor — the same stage-(iii) carrier-duality obstacle, compounded by an extra
     restriction-of-scalars wrapper, so `TensorProduct.smul_tmul'` does not apply directly.
  3. **The carrier-duality bridge is the stage-(iii) one.** Cross-referenced
     `\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}` (the `stalkTensorDescU_smul`/`stalkTensorLinearMap`
     step) as the identical canonical `RingEquiv` bridge for any unavoidable
     CommRingCat-vs-RingCat identification, so the reader sees the balancing reuses that
     resolved technique.

The existing five-stage structure, the stage (i)/(ii)/(iii)/(v) prose, and the Stacks
SOURCE QUOTE are untouched. No `\leanok`/`\mathlibok` markers added or removed.

## Cross-references introduced
- `\cref{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}` reused in stage (iv) prose — label confirmed present
  in this same chapter at the `\label{lem:stalk_tensor_linear_map}` block.

## References consulted
None — this is an expansion of an Archon-original / project-bespoke proof sketch (the
d.2 stalk–tensor commutation). No external citation block was added, so no `references/`
file was opened. The retained Stacks SOURCE QUOTE elsewhere in the lemma was left intact
and unmodified.

## Macros needed (if any)
None — only existing macros and `\mathtt{...}` typewriter notation already used throughout
the chapter.

## Notes for Plan Agent
- The prose names the Lean identifier `germ_smul` once (as the directive explicitly
  permitted) as the germ–scalar compatibility lemma; the surrounding text stands as
  mathematics. No other Lean tactic strings were introduced.
- Stage (iv) refers to `\mathtt{revBihom}` as the reverse bilinear map; this matches the
  identifier used in the prover-facing memory and the stage's existing nested-colimit
  description. If the formalized name diverges, only the typewriter token needs adjusting.

## Strategy-modifying findings
None.
